title: Setting Priorities: Deciding Which Patient to See First
subtitle: An RN starts the shift with four patients and must decide who to assess first. Using ABCs, Maslow, and unstable-before-stable, the nurse prioritizes a hypoxic COPD patient over routine and stable needs.

patient.name: Shift-Start Prioritization
patient.admitDate: RN receives report on four patients and must decide which to assess first, applying prioritization frameworks (ABCs, Maslow, acute/unstable before chronic/stable).

vitals.temp: Scheduled PO med — can be timed
Routine tasks first delays the hypoxic pt

labs.v: High-risk airway can outrank minor actual.

nursesNote: An RN takes report on four med-surg patients and must decide who to assess first. Patient A has COPD with a new SpO2 of 88%, rising respiratory rate, increasing dyspnea, and restlessness — an airway/breathing problem in an unstable patient. Patient B is post-op day 1 and is requesting a PRN pain medication; the need is real but the patient is stable. Patient C is stable and awaiting discharge teaching. Patient D has a scheduled 0900 oral medication that can be timed within its window. Applying ABCs, Maslow's hierarchy, and the principle that unstable/acute patients are seen before stable/chronic ones, the nurse assesses Patient A first, then addresses the stable pain need, gives the time-sensitive medication, and provides discharge teaching last. Priorities are reassessed continuously, since a sudden, unexpected change outranks an expected finding.
providerOrders: Obtain report and identify the least stable patient first
Apply ABCs and Maslow to prioritize the patient assignment
Assess the hypoxic patient before stable or routine tasks
Complete time-sensitive medications within their windows
Reassess and re-prioritize as patient conditions change

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: Which cues signal a higher-priority patient?

opts.text: A new SpO2 of 88% with rising dyspnea and restlessness is an airway/breathing priority
A scheduled 0900 oral medication can be timed within its window
Every patient who uses the call light must be seen before all others regardless of acuity
Restlessness in a hypoxic patient may signal early respiratory compromise
A stable patient awaiting discharge teaching is generally lower priority
Patients are prioritized purely by who asked first
ABCs — airway, breathing, circulation — come first
Maslow: physiologic needs come before psychosocial ones
Unstable/acute patients are seen before stable/chronic ones
A sudden, unexpected change outranks an expected, chronic finding
Localized complaints always outrank systemic problems
A high-risk potential airway problem can outrank a minor actual problem
Assess the COPD patient with SpO2 88% and worsening dyspnea
Medicate the stable post-op patient's pain
Give the 0900 scheduled oral medication within its window
Provide discharge teaching to the stable patient
Document and reassess, re-prioritizing as statuses change
New-onset hypoxia with restlessness in the COPD patient
A new run of ventricular tachycardia on telemetry
A routine 0900 oral vitamin
Stable patient awaiting discharge paperwork
Falling oxygen saturation unresponsive to current O2
A stable post-op patient requesting a PRN pain dose
A request for an extra blanket
Sudden chest pain with diaphoresis

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Get report and quickly identify the least stable patient
Assess the hypoxic COPD patient first (ABCs)
Address the stable post-op patient's pain need
Complete time-sensitive scheduled medications and routine care
Provide discharge teaching, then reassess and re-prioritize
Assessing the hypoxic COPD patient before the others
Using ABCs and Maslow to set priorities
Reassessing and re-prioritizing as conditions change
Seeing patients strictly in room-number order regardless of acuity
Delaying the hypoxic patient to first finish discharge teaching
Treating an expected chronic finding as urgently as a sudden change
Addressing the stable pain request after the airway emergency
Ignoring a new drop in oxygen saturation to stay on schedule

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Management of Care, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
